abstract: Factors influencing the intention to use telemedicine services among older adults in China Huiqian He1,2, Raja Ariffin Raja Ghazilla1,3 & Salwa Hanim Abdul‑Rashid1,3 The rapid growth of the older adult population has brought significant challenges to the medical industry. Problems such as uneven distribution of medical resources, low medical efficiency, and lack of post‑hospital care have become prominent. Many hospitals in China use online operating systems and the existing telemedicine service system does not consider the needs, preferences, and characteristics of older adults. Therefore, analyzing the factors that affect older adults’ intention to use telemedicine is essential for designing telemedicine service systems. The technology acceptance model (TAM) and emotional design theory were used to build a model of factors influencing older adults’ telemedicine use intention in China. This research gathers data via questionnaires and employs structural equation modelling for analysis. A total of 377 participants provided responses regarding telemedicine. The collected data was subsequently analyzed using structural equation modelling. Perceived usefulness and perceived ease of use are the primary drivers affecting the behavioral intention of older adults to use telemedicine, followed by cost value, system quality, trust, and self‑efficacy. In contrast, Technological anxiety has an adverse effect on the older adults’ intention to use telemedicine. These findings contribute to a validated model that plays a central role in explaining telemedicine adoption among older adults. The model provides actionable insights for policymakers, healthcare providers, and designers to support the development of inclusive service strategies, improve system accessibility, and enhance user‑centered design. It also offers a theoretical foundation for future research on sustained digital healthcare engagement in aging populations. Keywords Telemedicine, Older adults, Technology acceptance model, Emotional design theory, Structural equation modeling The rapid aging of the population and increasing life expectancy have created a huge market for healthcare service
conclusion: Conclusion This study makes a novel contribution by integrating the Technology Acceptance Model (TAM) with affective design principles to examine the factors influencing older adults’ intention to use telemedicine services in China. By extending the traditional TAM framework to include emotional and user-centered variables such as trust, self-efficacy, technology anxiety, and system quality, the study offers a more comprehensive understanding of telemedicine adoption. The proposed model highlights both cognitive and affective determinants, emphasizing that emotional factors play a critical role alongside perceived usefulness and ease of use in shaping user intentions. Moreover, the study identifies key barriers preventing telemedicine use among older adults, including distrust, perceived complexity, technological anxiety, and learning difficulties. By addressing these issues, it provides actionable design strategies to enhance system usability, emotional comfort, and accessibility. This study aids technology developers in gaining a deeper understanding of the elderly’s behavior patterns, encourage medical practitioners to pay attention to telemedicine systems, and improve the quality, ease of use, and usability of their systems. These findings provide reference value for telemedicine technology developers, system designers, medical personnel and governments, and provide a direction for promoting the use of telemedicine systems by older adults. Based on these findings, design specifications are proposed for designers and developers. First, it is crucial to enhance the utility and ease of use of telemedicine for older adults, reduce learning time and cognitive barriers, and ensure that telemedicine services are covered by medical insurance. In addition, it is important to boost service quality in terms of information and interaction, and elevate medical and expert credentialing to build trust among older adults. It is recommended that technology support, privacy security, and individual service should be provided to encourage older adults to use telemedicine and increase their self-efficacy. Trainings should be offered to improve system resilience and alleviate technological anxiety. In the future, it is also necessary to enhance the adaptability of telemedicine service scenarios, establish a collaborative network between communities and families for support, promote telemedicine through multiple channels, and strengthen policy support and social security. Future studies can be carried out to analyze and compare telemedicine usage and the factors that influence the intention to use telemedicine between older adults in China and Western countries, providing insights into the global telemedicine market for older adults. Future research also can explore the dynamic and longitudinal changes in older adults’ adoption of telemedicine, including shifts in attitudes and behaviors over time. Expanding the study to diverse regions and socio-economic groups across China would enhance the generalizability of the proposed model. Integrating additional theoretical frameworks, such as the Health Belief Model (HBM), may offer deeper insights into the psychological, cultural, and contextual factors influencing telemedicine use. Interdisciplinary collaboration with designers, engineers, and healthcare professionals is also encouraged to support the development of user-centered telemedicine interfaces that are both emotionally responsive and technologically
